q: The two steps in protein synthesis are transcription and translation.
Transcription involves the production of a protein with an amino acid sequence based on the nucleic acid sequence of the mRNA.
The central dogma of molecular biology stated that genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to Protein.
Transcription occurs in the nucleus of the cell.
There are three defined stages in the transcription process: initiation, elongation, and termination.
For each gene, both strands of the double-stranded DNA molecule are transcribed to produce mRNA.
The DNA strand that is transcribed is called the antisense strand or template strand.
The sense strand or coding strand has the same sequence as the product mRNA, with thymine instead of uracil.
During elongation, the RNA polymerase complex synthesizes a strand of mRNA that is complementary to the template strand of DNA in $5'$ to $3'$ direction.
Termination occurs when the RNA polymerase complexes reach the termination signal and detach from the DNA strand.
The newly synthesized mRNA is called mature mRNA before any modifications occur.
Modifications convert pre-mRNA to mature mRNA through the addition of a $5'$ cap and a $3'$ poly-A tail.
Removal of introns (non-coding regions) and joining of exons (coding regions) is part of the mRNA modification process.
A poly-A tail consisting of 100-200 adenine nucleotides is added after transcription.
Splicing involves snRNA forming base-pairs with the $5'$ end of an intron and at the branch site to form a spliceosome.
Translation occurs in the cytoplasm and involves the conversion of a sequence of nucleotides into a sequence of amino acids.
There are two subunits in the ribosome: the small subunit and the large subunit.
There are three binding sites for tRNA on the large subunit of ribosome: A site, P site and E site.
Each tRNA carries the specific amino acid at a time at its acceptor stem.
The anticodon on tRNA connects to the specific codon on the mRNA.
In the initiation phase of translation, the first tRNA that binds to the codon is the initiator tRNA with its UAC anticodon.
During elongation, a tRNA with an attached polypeptide is in the A site, then a peptide bond is formed and the next amino acid enters the P site.
The termination phase of translation begins when a stop codon on the mRNA is reached.
A protein called a release factor cleaves (cuts) the polypeptide from the last tRNA during termination.
